title.emphasis: Part III: Property, Public Good, and the Power of the State

section.para: State courts are the primary arbiters of the fundamental tension in American property law: the conflict between property as a private right of exclusion and property as a public resource subject to regulation for the "general welfare." Their decisions profoundly impact who can live where, what industries can operate, and who benefits from scientific progress.

title.emphasis: Section 3.1: Confronting Exclusionary Zoning and Defining "Public Use"

section.para: In one of the most significant land use decisions in American history, the New Jersey Supreme Court in Southern Burlington County NAACP v. Township of Mount Laurel, 67 N.J. 151 (1975), confronted the issue of exclusionary zoning. The Township of Mount Laurel had enacted zoning ordinances that effectively made it impossible to build housing for low- and moderate-income families by, for example, permitting only single-family detached dwellings on large lots.69 The court held that a developing municipality could not use its state-delegated zoning power to foreclose the opportunity for such housing. Citing the state constitution's "general welfare" clause, the court established an affirmative obligation for every municipality to create a realistic opportunity for the construction of its "fair share" of the region's present and prospective need for low- and moderate-income housing. This "Mount Laurel Doctrine" was unique in its scope and has led to decades of litigation and legislation in New Jersey aimed at dismantling residential segregation.11

In stark contrast to the rights-affirming approach of Mount Laurel, the Michigan Supreme Court in Poletown Neighborhood Council v. City of Detroit, 410 Mich. 616 (1981), greatly expanded the state's power of eminent domain. The City of Detroit, facing severe economic decline, used its condemnation power to acquire a 465-acre residential neighborhood known as Poletown, which it then sold to General Motors for the construction of a new assembly plant.72 The displaced residents argued that this was an unconstitutional taking of private property for a private use. The court, however, held that the project served a "public purpose" by alleviating unemployment and revitalizing the city's economic base.74 This controversial decision, which found that the benefit to a private corporation was incidental to the public good, became a leading precedent for the use of eminent domain for private economic development. The decision was eventually overruled by the same court in 2004, but it foreshadowed the national debate that would later culminate in the U.S. Supreme Court's decision in
Kelo v. City of New London.72

title.emphasis: Section 3.2: Resolving Novel Questions of Property and Nuisance

section.para: The rise of biotechnology presented courts with novel and profound questions about property rights. In Moore v. Regents of the University of California, 51 Cal.3d 120 (1990), a leukemia patient, John Moore, discovered that his doctor had used his excised spleen cells to develop a patented and commercially valuable "cell line" without his knowledge or consent.76 Moore sued, claiming the doctors had converted his personal property. The California Supreme Court rejected this claim, holding that a patient does not retain a property interest in their excised cells sufficient to support a claim for conversion.78 The court feared that recognizing such a right would have a "chilling effect" on socially important medical research. However, the court did find that the physicians had breached their fiduciary duty and failed to obtain informed consent by not disclosing their research and economic interests in Moore's cells. The decision navigated the complex ethical and legal intersection of patient rights, scientific progress, and commercialization.77

In Boomer v. Atlantic Cement Co., 26 N.Y.2d 219 (1970), the New York Court of Appeals confronted a classic industrial-era conflict. A large cement plant was found to be a nuisance, causing significant property damage to neighboring landowners from dirt, smoke, and vibration. However, the plant represented a $45 million investment and employed over 300 people.79 Under traditional nuisance law, the landowners would be entitled to an injunction shutting down the plant. The court, however, refused to issue an injunction, citing the "large disparity in economic consequences" between the harm to the plaintiffs ($185,000) and the economic impact of closing the plant. Instead, it fashioned a novel remedy: awarding permanent damages to the plaintiffs to compensate them for the total economic loss to their property. This decision effectively allowed the plant to pay for the right to continue polluting and marked a major shift toward a "balancing of the equities" approach in modern environmental nuisance law.79

title.emphasis: Section 3.3: Preserving Customary and Traditional Rights

section.para: State courts are uniquely positioned to integrate local history and culture into their jurisprudence. In Public Access Shoreline Hawaii (PASH) v. Hawaii County Planning Commission, 79 Haw. 424 (1995), the Hawaii Supreme Court addressed the conflict between modern property development and the rights of native Hawaiians. The case involved a challenge to a permit for a resort development on coastal land that had been traditionally used by native Hawaiians for subsistence, cultural, and religious purposes.82 The court held that the Hawaii Constitution requires state agencies to "preserve and protect" traditional and customary native Hawaiian rights to the extent feasible. It ruled that these rights, which include gathering and access, may extend to privately owned land and must be considered in land use and zoning decisions. The
PASH decision is a powerful example of a state court weaving unique cultural and historical rights into its modern property and administrative law framework.84
